---
title: "C++ Notes"
author: "Ray Erickson"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  bookdown::gitbook:
    css: style.css
    split_by: none
    split_bib: false
    config:
      documentclass: report
      link-citations: yes
      toc_depth: 3
      number_section: false
      number_chapter: false
      toc:
        collapse: section
      code_folding: hide # have check and see if get anoyed when noting code chunks
      toolbar:
        position: fixed
      info: false
      sharing: null
      download: [["book.pdf", "Download PDF"]]
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: none

---

<!--![title](pngs/name.png)-->

# C++ Notes {.tabset}

start programs with `#include <library>` to add libraries to call functions from

## Terms

- most statements require **`;`** after function
- **`//`** single line comment
- **`/* ...*/`** multiline comment
- a *value* is a single piece of data
- an *object* is a stored value
- a *variable* is a named object


## Key List

- `#include` is a preprocessor directive typically used to call a library
- `iostream` — part of c++ library that allows to read/write text from console
- `=` is assignment operator
- `==` is equals
- `std::cout` *"character output"* print data on output device
- `std::cin` read input from keyboard - is buffered
- `<<` insertion operator, can be repeated to link multiple outputs ex. text and a function
- `>>` extraction operator
- `std::endl` ends line, operates as a return, else outputs are on the same line - useful to denote end of thought not just to break up lines. - flushes the buffer so can be quite slow.
- `\n` newline character, must be put in either single quotes or double quotes, useful for putting in existing quoted text 
- `[[maybe_unused]]` attribute allows to tell compiler is fine variable not in use so not throw error




## Functions {.tabset}

A function is a collection of statements that get executed sequentially (in order, from top to bottom).

> Every c++ program must have a special function named `main` (all lower case letters).

When the program is run, the statements inside of `main` are executed in sequential order.

*Programs typically terminate after the last statement inside function main has been executed (though programs may abort early in some circumstances, or do some cleanup afterwards).*

you can put multiple inputs on the same line, but they need to be separated by some white space *(space, tab, enter)*, space or tab preferable

### Initialization {.tabset}

#### Definition 

After a variable has been defined, you can give it a value (in a separate statement) using the `=` operator. This process is called assignment, and the `=` operator is called the assignment operator.

```
#include <iostream>

int main()
{
	int width; // define a variable named width
	width = 5; // copy assignment of value 5 into variable width

	std::cout << width; // prints 5

	width = 7; // change value stored in variable width to 7

	std::cout << width; // prints 7

	return 0;
}
```


Initialization combines these two into one. When an object is defined, you can optionally provide an initial value for the object. The process of specifying an initial value for an object is called initialization, and the syntax used to initialize an object is called an initializer. Informally, the initial value is often called an “initializer” as well.

```
#include <iostream>

int main()
{
    int width { 5 };    // define variable width and initialize with initial value 5
    std::cout << width; // prints 5

    return 0;
}
```



```
int a;         // default-initialization (no initializer)

// Traditional initialization forms:
int b = 5;     // copy-initialization (initial value after equals sign)
int c ( 6 );   // direct-initialization (initial value in parenthesis)

// Modern initialization forms (preferred):
int d { 7 };   /* direct-list-initialization
 (initial value in braces) prefered*/

int e {};      // value-initialization (empty braces)

int a = 5, b = 6;          // copy-initialization
int c ( 7 ), d ( 8 );      // direct-initialization
int e { 9 }, f { 10 };     // direct-list-initialization
int i {}, j {};            // value-initialization

/* must have some initializer for both, 
cannot initialize both with one number*/
```

#### Types

##### List-initialization (preferred)

The modern way to initialize objects in C++ is to use a form of initialization that makes use of curly braces. This is called list-initialization (or uniform initialization or brace initialization).

List-initialization comes in two forms: direct-list, and copy-list

includes "narrowing conversions" that make an error message when trying to list initialize a value that the variable cannot hold.


##### Default-initialization

When no initializer is provided, this is called default-initialization. In many cases, default-initialization performs no initialization, and leaves the variable with an indeterminate value (a value that is not predictable, sometimes called a “garbage value”)


##### Copy-initialization

When an initial value is provided after an equals sign, this is called copy-initialization. This form of initialization was inherited from the C language.

##### Direct-initialization

When an initial value is provided inside parenthesis, this is called direct-initialization.

##### Value-initialization (Zero-initialization)

When a variable is initialized using an empty set of braces, a special form of list-initialization called value-initialization takes place. In most cases, value-initialization will implicitly initialize the variable to zero (or whatever value is closest to zero for a given type). In cases where zeroing occurs, this is called zero-initialization.

##### Aggregate-initialization


##### Reference initialization

##### Static-initialization

##### Constant-initialization

##### Dynamic-initialization


### Statements {.tabset}

##### Declaration statements

##### Jump statements

##### Expression statements

##### Compound statements

##### Selection statements (conditionals)

##### Iteration statements (loops)

##### Try blocks
